era_context: Classical Greece in the mid-5th century BCE, during the First Peloponnesian War (460–445 BCE) and the height of Athenian imperial expansion. The Delian League is at its zenith. In China, the Spring and Autumn period is in its final decades. In Mesoamerica, Classic Maya civilization continues to flourish.

events.title: Battle of the Eurymedon River (traditional dating)
events.region: Pamphylia, southern coast of Asia Minor
events.description: A major naval and land battle between Athenian forces (under Cimon) and the Persian fleet and army. The Athenians achieve a decisive victory, destroying a large Persian fleet and defeating the accompanying land forces. This victory is celebrated as one of the greatest Athenian triumphs and is said to have secured Athenian dominance in the Aegean and eastern Mediterranean for a generation. However, the exact date of this battle is disputed among modern scholars; some place it as early as 466 BCE, while others suggest dates in the 450s BCE. The battle marks a high point of Athenian imperial power.
key_figures: Themistocles (possibly involved in earlier phases, though his role by 456 BCE is unclear)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1

events.certainty_note: The battle is well-attested in ancient sources, but the precise date is highly disputed. Ancient sources give varying dates; modern scholarship ranges from 466 to 450 BCE. The traditional date of 456 BCE is one of several plausible options. No contemporary inscription or primary source directly dates the battle.
cross_references: Precedes Peace of Callias (~449 BCE); part of broader Delian League expansion

events.title: Athenian operations in the Aegean and eastern Mediterranean
events.description: Athens continues to consolidate control over the Delian League, suppressing revolts and expanding tribute collection. Naxos, which had rebelled, is brought back under Athenian control. These operations reflect the transformation of the Delian League from a voluntary alliance into an Athenian empire. The period 460–450 BCE sees intense Athenian military activity across the eastern Mediterranean.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1

events.certainty_note: The broad pattern of Athenian expansion is well-attested; specific operations in 456 BCE are less clearly documented. Thucydides provides a general chronology, but precise dating of individual campaigns is uncertain.
cross_references: Part of broader Delian League consolidation

events.title: Aeschylus' final plays and death
events.description: Aeschylus, the great Athenian tragedian, is in the final years of his life (he dies in 456 or 455 BCE, according to tradition). His later plays, including works in the Oresteia trilogy, represent the height of Greek tragic drama. Aeschylus had fought at Marathon and witnessed the Persian Wars; his plays reflect the values and anxieties of Classical Athens. His death marks the end of an era in Greek drama, though Sophocles and Euripides continue to dominate the stage.
events.certainty_note: Aeschylus' death date is traditionally given as 456 or 455 BCE, but the exact year is uncertain. Ancient sources vary; some place his death in Sicily. The date is approximate within a 1–2 year range.
cross_references: Part of Classical Athenian cultural flourishing

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of the Eurymedon is one of the most chronologically disputed events in Classical Greek history. Modern scholars have proposed dates ranging from 466 to 450 BCE, with some arguing for multiple separate engagements conflated into a single 'battle' in later sources. The traditional date of 456 BCE is not strongly supported by contemporary evidence and may reflect later historiographic convention rather than historical fact. Additionally, the extent of Athenian naval dominance after this battle is sometimes overstated in ancient sources; Persian naval power recovered significantly in subsequent decades.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 456 BCE is heavily dependent on later Greek historians, particularly Thucydides and Plutarch, writing 50+ years after the events. No contemporary inscriptions directly document the Battle of the Eurymedon or its date. Persian sources are almost entirely lost. The narrative is shaped by Athenian imperial ideology and the need to construct a coherent chronology from fragmentary sources. Modern scholars have increasingly questioned the reliability of ancient dating for this period.

graph_edges.note: Athenian victory at Eurymedon (if dated to 456 BCE) contributes to negotiating position for peace with Persia
Part of broader Athenian imperial expansion
Aeschylus' final years coincide with Periclean cultural flourishing
